if you ever feel downif you ever feel like giving upif you ever feel like nothing is gonna workoutif you ever feel hopelessif you ever feel like dyingwatch this film it is a miraculous medicine it is a wonderful movie hands down the shawshank redemption is unquestionably the most overrated film in the entire history of motion pictures75100 a profound slow burn prison drama that gets excellently crafted and narrated dealing with themes of hope faith freedom friendship and redemption the cinematography and music stand out keeping you engaged throughout for a movie majorly happening at one single place the plot utilizes its cinematic liberty with convenient coincidences however the narrative does build its tension subtly culminating into one of the highly satisfying and deeply moving third act evertim robbinsandmorgan freemandeliver outstanding performances with the latters voiceover providing a powerful impact highly recommended watchhope is a good thing maybe the best of things and no good thing ever dies get busy livin or get busy dyin redthere i watched it i am now a free man it was good great excellent in fact still overrated but could it be anything else my heart lies firmly withpulp fictionand no amount of oh its the greatest film ever cause imdb says so could make it anything other than a disappointment but ive been disappointed far more violently than thisall the critical acclaim i can see ismore my favorite movie of all time pure mater piece phenomenal

i know its a broad question but the 90s to me always used to be a mixed decade sure every decade has its ups and downs and you just need to know where to look but lately ive started to turn around on my opinion maybe its because i grew up in the 90s so it took some distance some get away and appreciate what was going on but for all the dated poor effects blockbusters like godzilla or spawn theres la confidential or the shawshank redemption even the dreck of certain teen comedies was somewhat offset by the arrival of richard linklater and kevin smith so i guess to get more specific do you think the 90s were a good decade for cinema which films do you think will be remembered for decades to come what were the styles and trends that changed the cinematic landscape or defined the decade for better or worse not limited to just good or bad but guiltynot guilty like in shawshank redemption dead man walking or death the maiden what were some times when the main character was set up to be good but turned out bad or vice versa or like when who they really are is a mystery whether thats good or bad why did you likedislike those characters its been about five years since i watched the shawshank redemption and what strikes me most about it is how restrained the story telling is some have said it drags especially the second half but i cant really see where the seconds could be shaved off i want a story that takes its time the action and the pacing is in their faces in the minds of these men watching them cope with their incarceration and what it does tomore film ofproject 90let me tell you something my friend hope is a dangerous thing hope can drive a man insanewhythe shawshank redemptionis such a popular movie it is wellmade and wellpaced tim robbins and morgan freeman are perfect in their roles and frank darabonts ability of narrating the story without hiccups and his charming way of using multiple supporting characters to add more depth to the story of his two protagonists make the experiencemore in shawshank prison the inmates are doing serious time through the eyes of prisoner red we follow the story of andy dufresne who comes to shawshank in 1947 for murdering his wife and her lover red and andy become friends based on a story by stephen king the life in prison is a metaphor to real society the prisoners settle for a life at shawshank they stop dreaming so nothing can get worse than it already is the big guysmore remember red hope is a good thing maybe the best of things and no good thing ever diesfinally i dont no why it took me so long to see one of the most acclaimed films of all timethe shawshank redemptionreally moved methe emotion that was put into it was something really truthful and real even when there are moments where you predict what is going to happen next its subtleness and immense depth affects you anywaymore

i was having a conversation with one of my buddies and we found ourselves stuck at one point comparing movies i personally believe that each film can be compared to films within its own genre i think comparing movies outside of their own genres can be a disservice to each film like comparing jurassic park to shawshank redemption which was the topic of our discussion he believes that each movie can be compared to any other movie regardless of genre while i dont disagree i find it extremely hard for me to do as every genre has something different to offer for me i would just like to get any feedback and opinion on this the list of greatest films ever made whether it be sight sound the afi top 100 or even something like the imdb top 250 is always changing as new critically acclaimed films join the list we see older maybe not as well regarded films leave it more recently and perhaps most infamously we saw vertigo overtake citizen kane the de facto greatest movie ever made on the 2012 sight sound pole this is probably more shocking because back in during its theatrical release vertigo was not that well regarded it earned less than hitchcocks previous films and reviews were mixed at best in fact there are more than a couple of films on these lists that werent wellreceived or that were overlooked upon their initial release but have since gone on to become classics its a wonderful life blade runner and the shining to name just a few hell half the films on the criterion collection have that kind of reputation but time and critical reevaluation have been kinder to them the french famously reevaluated many films starting in the 1950s and it seems the movie brat generation consisting of martin scorsese steven spielberg and george lucas among others helped bring the forgotten or overlooked films of john ford akira kurosawa and powell pressburger to the audiences and film courses around the world by acknowledging the effects they had on their own films and so they too were added to the canon of great films but what happens when a critically reevaluated movies gets reevaluated again i feel over the past few years ive started to notice a backlash against films like blade runner the searchers the shawshank redemption and even the recently appointed vertigo films that once seemed to have an assured spot at the top but now im not so sure yes there are people out there who think its cool to hate classic films or that know that an article discrediting a great film will get more views than one praising it but what about people who actually think these films are no longer worthy of the acclaim theyve been given over the past twenty thirty or forty years on the one hand it makes sense certain films mean something to a certain generation and a certain time as they grow up and start to take influence in the industry those films will also gain influence until another generation comes along and replaces it as the years go on and film history becomes represented by centuries as opposed to decades if it lasts that long the movies we cling to will change even now if i chose ten different people to teach a film class i feel they could produce different curriculum with little to no overlap people complain when rmovies votes films like pulp fiction and fight club over citizen kane and lawrence of arabia but what if those are the movies that will redefine the standard for great film in cinemas second century what do you guys think is it possible that in a few years these films will not only fall off the list of greatest films ever made but possibly not i recently watched the netflix original arq which i loved im a huge fan of dystopian scifi so i may be a little biased anyway i decided to check out the ratings on imdb and i saw that out of about 7900 ratings it had a rating of 65 that seems like a fair rating of the movie given that its not everyones cup of tea of course we could have the common discussion about how reliable ratings from imdb are the general consensus seems to be not very reliable i however typically find them pretty useful my main point though is that the ratings themselves make no sense to me not the average rating but the breakdown 19 of the total ratings 1547913 at this moment are 110 for the life of me i cant even begin to understand how someone can give this movie a 1 much less 154 people i get that opinions are subjective but for someone to give this a 1 would have to mean that it is on their very bottom tier for films which i dont understand if you look at the shawshank redemption the breakdown has 17 of the ratings being 110 12 angry men has a breakdown with 15 of the ratings being 110 again opinions can be subjective but what reason would anyone have to vote these movies a 1 i can understand a ratings worse than a 10 or 9 or 8 or 7 but a 1 i guess the question i want to pose is why do you think these films have ratings like this is it a problem with user rating systems do you believe the ridiculously low ratings to be serious or do you believe them to be more sarcastic or possibly driven by personal agendas also what is your criteria for rating a movie a 1 out of the 370 movies that i have rated on imdb i have only given a single movie a 110 a haunted house and even now i think that maybe deserves a 2 or 3 what do you guys think so ive seen this device used over and over and over and im under the impression that it is meaningful and significant my favorite example is back the the future 1 where humble nerd george mcfly knocks aggressive bully biff tannens block off they then undergo a reversal of fortune the new future that marty helped create sees george as a selfactualized and exuberant creator and biff as some servile chump slumdog millionaire has a similar example but this has its roots in biblical narratives jesus died as a lamb but will return as a lion jesus rode into jerusalem the first time on a donkey humbled and ridiculed the second time he will be riding majestic white steed the first time he came to die at the hands of the military the second time he stamps the militaries of the world under his feet and rules the entire planet with a rod of iron the first time he came unnoticed the second time every eye will see him i think shawshank redemption really played this device well also but what are some more example of charactersnarratives that use this device also is there a special academicliterary name for it are there books written about it please help edit stuff when i watch films with people who arent as adoring of cinema as myself our discussions postviewing are almost always about the message or meaning of the film wow that movie really made me think about x shawshank redemption or what was the point of that movie knight of cups are examples of this where the viewer determines their opinion of the film more on the content of the narrative rather than the way in which it is shown personally i could care less what the story was about i go to the cinema to be immersed in another world or space to be somewhere ive never been to experience something new the narrative is only important in that it must be believable in the universe that the film resides in what are your thoughts on this do you focus on the purpose or the experience of film or are both of these equally important theres a growing attitude recently that blockbuster films that were cut down for theatrical release will somehow get better once their directors cut versions will be released this isnt that far off from the similar argument that things not finished or explained in one film will be explained in the sequel and it comes from a similar flawed point of view first off id like to point out that yes directors cuts and alternate cuts can greatly improve on a lacking theatrical cut perhaps theres no better an example than ridley scotts kingdom of heaven which endures a dull tedium in its theatrical cut but becomes a magical grand epic in its directors cut but not every movie is kingdom of heaven the things will be fixed in the directors cut is a lazy way of dismissing criticisms that exist in the theatrical cut that we all paid to see if we are to buy that a directors cut or sequel will somehow fix all the problems by giving us a newer and better version to watch why are we paying to see the barely functional theatrical version instead this is the cinematic form of what gamers call dlc perhaps these stories as presented would benefit from a longer running time to tell them in but then why bother in the first place movies have been around for over a hundred years and quite a lot of them have told compelling detailed and nuanced stories in under two hours batman v superman tells a story that supposedly takes place over the course of a year and is 2 hours and 31 minutes long the shawshank redemption tells a story that takes place over a couple decades and is 8 minutes shorter citizen kane details the entire life of a man and nearly all the nuances of his life in 1 hour and 59 minutes la confidential has a wide range of complex subplots that are all coherently told over the course of a swift 2 hours and 18 minutes none of these movies needed 3 hours or a miniseries to tell a great story theres a reason they tell you to kill your darlings and that brevity is the soul of wit sometimes shorter is better sure longer movies can be awesome one of the best recent filmgoing experiences i had was seeing a restoration of akira kurosawas ran but it wasnt great because it was long it was great because akira kurosawa was a master storyteller and he knew how best to use the audiences time it was why he could make long films like seven samurai and ran yet still make equal masterpieces out of films like rashomon and yojimbo zack snyder and duncan jones arent akira kurosawa they have their talents in some areas sure but theyre not particularly great storytellers and even if their directors cuts improve on their theatrical counterparts not much can be changed in how these directors view storytelling in general yet batman v superman hadnt even been released when talk was made of its inevitable ultimate cut warcraft hasnt even opened in the us yet and there are talks about 40 minutes that were cut that will make everything better and we dont even know the specifics of those 40 minutes which might frankly be filler this isnt like when ridley scott went back to kingdom of heaven after it had been released and failed to show everyone the movie he really wanted to make this is pure cynical hollywood marketing put out a heavily flawed version the fans will see anyways and deliver the supposedly better movie later for them to buy its total bullshit itd be one thing if these were promised directors cut that add a lot for fans much like the extended editions of lord of the rings but if you cant bother to get it right the first time why bother making a movie at all so the directors guild just surveyed their members on the greatest directed films celebrating 80 years of the organization establishment of course the list gets published and pretty much signifies how much hollywood directors dont know much about anything about the craft much less film history only six foreign titles showed up on the list of those sixth four were italian filmsthe bicycle thieves 8 12 cinema paradiso the conformist two were kurosawa films that means many global cinema giants were ignored on this list in favor for generic middlebrow prestige films the shawshank redemption and forrest gump are on the list shawshank being ranked higher than any film directed by hitchcock would you really consider the direction for casablanca to be of significance greatness consider curtiz was nothing more than a studio handyman who went in direct and called it a day likewise it seems bogus to have gone with the wind and wizard of oz on the list when they were codirected by three directors one of which was victor fleming people still rate the sting and gandhi that highly all hollywood narrative films with no documentaries or avantgarde films on the list no film without the backing of major hollywood studio very lacking in diversity with just one woman and no noted person of color behind the camera the snubbing of many great american directors hawks mann minnelli malick lynch fuller many more here was the list published by dga which only 137 members participated in the website imdbcom generates a list of the top 250 movies based on user ratings and their own algorithm the list has problems for sure theres been significant attempts to game the system promoting and demoting certain movies right now and for the long forseeable future the shawshank redemption is at on the list whether that movie actually deserves to be on the top 250 movies list is suspect but despite its flaws the top 250 does showcase a great collection of interesting important worthwhile and fun movies imdb is both relatively open and relatively opaque on how they create the top 250 list they speak in detail on how the list is created and provide lots of data on user ratings but also only use a subset of top users to determine ratings the makeup of whom is not entirely clear as i have been going through the list watching movies in an attempt to watch all the top 250 im currently 4 movies away i thought it would be very interesting to gather the data imdb provides in a spreadsheet where ratings and other information can be crunched relatively to each other there are countless interesting results that could be determined if all of this information was in one place which movies have the highest or lowest 10s which movies are rated highest by nonus users which movies are enjoyed most by women or men and what ages gravitate to which movies which movies rankings are the most controversial the list goes on and on to create this list seems almost insurmountable there are 250 movies on the list and entering the stats takes about 2 minutes per movie that would take one person over 8 hours so i thought i would present this spreadsheet to my fellow redditors and see who would like to contribute to the project if each person reading this picked a couple movies to add the rating information this spreadsheet would be completed in no time available to whomever would find the data interesting and would only take a few minutes of everyones time if you would like to contribute pick a couple of your favorite movies and navigate to their imdb pages links provided on the actual spreadsheet at the top of the movies page you will see the rating for the movie between 80 92 directly underneath the rating is the number of people that have rated the film if you click that number it will take you to a page that has all the data that needs to be inputted to the spreadsheet the columns of the spreadsheet are organized to follow the order of information on the site so it is easier to enter data once the spreadsheet is fully fulled out i will go through and come up with as many interesting results as i can with the data and everyone is welcome to do the same you can i feel like ive seen the phrase it was technically excellent but more and more on reddit in their words its as if people just completely dismiss the technical aspects of filmmaking they get all that aside in maybe a sentence but spend the meat of their comment or review talking about how the story was convoluted or that it didnt make you feel for the characters or what have you i think people at large undervalue the technical aspects of films unless theyre innovative masterpieces like jurassic park in favor of almost exclusively seeing them as a medium of storytelling a lot of people seem to think that if a film isnt the shawshank redemption of action movies or something it isnt worthy of substantial praise my issue with this thinking is that film is not simply a storytelling vehicle but an art form people dont criticize paintings for not being the most emotional thing theyve seen books are more than just the plot the presentation and style matter just as much if not more than plot and characterization not to say that those things arent important or that they cant be the thing that makes a film great there are plenty of examples from to kill a mockingbird to nightcrawler where the characters and story are enough to make the film a masterpiece but a technically brilliant film with light or somewhat lackluster storytelling can be just as great and should be viewed as such are we just going to dismiss films like samsara or 2001 a space odyssey where theres little story but the execution is outstanding in every way i want to discuss two recent examples of what im talking about mad max fury road and dunkirk while the former certainly got a lot of praise there were also many who dismissed it as just a fun action movie in particular andy signore from screen junkies criticized it for having no story my question to these people is do you realize just how hard it is to make action movies that are as good as this would you prefer it if immorton joe sat down for five minutes and explained how he got in power and why hes such a bad dude for one thing i certainly wouldnt there are so many action movies that waste valuable screen time explaining a plot that isnt that deep or even that important i would much rather it be done like fury road where theres just enough story to make it not just a collection of set pieces as for dunkirk people have criticized it for not spending enough time on the characterization to make it more dramatic while i suppose that could be true i dont think that thats what nolan was going for i think he wanted to put the audience into the dunkirk operation and make them feel that fear that terror that hopelessness he wanted to sell the audience on the aesthetic and feeling of war and he did so not through focusing on character and plot but on pacing editing cinematography and sound design and i think people missed that because they wanted saving private ryan instead of something wholly unique and fantastically executed anyways those preface i 10 years of films a decadelong journey of programming starting 2007 through 2017 gave this filmmaker a special lens to witness the comingofage of korean american cinema kaffny korean american film festival new york was not originally from an impetus of my own but out of a request by others to do something for the community when a lofty vision of a huge festival in flushing to commemorate the 100 years of koreanamerican immigration was heavily watered down relocating our film screening to the basement of dae dong palace a friend and i quickly maneuvered to secure a venue at anthology film archives to save our reputations the turnout that year and fresh possibilities in subsequent years has kept me volunteering until today now passing it on to newer visionaries unfairly but understandably i always have to explain that korean american cinema is not korean cinema nor a subset of asian american cinema as a student educated in critical race theory it is obvious to me that the term asian american is a political term branded after the african american civil rights movement without that understanding most people assume these are objective categories which neatly capture the infinite range of culture however even america as it is semantically defined in todays culture hardly captures the breadth of cultural interchange that takes place here beyond a liberal and conservative estimation by those in the perceived majority who control our mainstream communications both directly and indirectly when is the last time we had a president that speaks more than english which by the way is the language of our colonial masters england when is the last time anyone considered that the term america actually captures all of this entire hemisphere ie the new world and why do we celebrate columbus day when this nation is named after amerigo vespucci it should be obvious america is a concept much bigger than solely the united states of america merely by finally perceiving the cultural prisons in which we find ourselves doesnt automatically and suddenly extricate you in fact it begins a long inescapable nightmare the oppressed and the oppressors alike becoming unwitting zombie actors one oppressing out of fear one in fear of more oppression not unlike the stanford prison experiment the final step is a process of understanding the prisons weaknesses balanced against your own learned abilities in order to implement a practical means of neardeath escape for yourself and perhaps others like a shawshank redemption which most of us shall never taste anyway while cinema has been used to oppress and brainwash like any tool it can easily be used for good to express the virtues of humanity to powerfully smash walls i find it is more meaningful and accurate to depict ourselves as a means for understanding the interaction between cultures outside of the colonial lens which is not often demonstrated at even the most prestigious international festivals expending any precious volunteer energy to form some reactionary identity against an arbitrary fickle enemy is a enervating distraction with the symptoms when one could rather proactively highlight the cultural physics of the natural world such vision of korean american cinema represents horizontallynetworked cultural relationship cinema not verticallysegregated cultural identity politics i korean american frontier phillip ahn 19051978 is the first korean american born in the us and the first and most successful korean american actor in hollywood history with hundreds of credits on many american classics working with stars like bing crosby in anything goes 1936 shirley temple in stowaway 1936 elvis presley in paradise hawaiian style 1966 as well as john wayne gary cooper humphrey bogart burt lancaster frank sinatra key luke richard loo ethel merman julie andrews and mae west phillip would be given his star on hollywoods walk of fame in 1984 the first for an asian american film actor ahn also starred in the independent film daughter of shanghai 1937 opposite chinese american trailblazer and childhood friend anna may wong ahn is also the first son of dosan ahn changho a famous korean patriot who devoted his life to winning korean independence from japanese colonial rule ahns parents were the first couple to immigrate from korea to america and his mother helen was the second korean woman to arrive these pre1924 immigrants selftermed as the korean american pioneer generation ahn too was also civicminded one of his major achievements bringing the massive bronze korean friendship bell to san pedro establishing busan as a sister city to los angeles ahns mother first refused to accept his interest in acting even after famed director douglas fairbanks offered him a job during high school from an impromptu screen test when his father later visited from korea he saw the value in the art of acting and gave his blessing as long as ahn made it his goal to be the best ahn also drew some controversy for playing evil japanese characters for world war ii propaganda but he saw it as his contribution to the korean cause his last role would be playing master kan in the tv series kung fu 1972 after ahn there were no new faces in korean american cinema until long after 1965 when the racial quotas set in the asian exclusion act of 1924 were lifted the asian exclusion act of 1924 is a major feature of korean american cinema placing an artificial 40year time divide between immigrants coming before 1924 and those coming after the 1965 repeal during the civil rights era in the us the law stopped in its tracks the process of assimilation for the korean american community and is perhaps the worst single act against the its cinema in fact if there was no asian exclusion act one might never have expected the los angeles riots in 1992 to rock the korean american community so hard where korean american immigrants were portrayed in popular media as foreign invaders economically preying on lowincome african americans in the area of south central la now called south la pioneer korean americans who came to los angeles in the early 1900s were segregated into the same areas with african americans who were refugees arriving from the midwest dust bowl agricultural tragedy the early koreans only remembered a unified korea while todays immigrants know a korea divided into north and south the korean united presbyterian church was built by the pioneer generation on west jefferson boulevard in south la in 1905 on august 15th every year they continue to celebrate the day korea won independence from japan in 1945 in contrast post1965 immigrants remember and observe april 29th as the date of the riots in 1992 illustrating the artificial divide between these communities the asian exclusion act of 1924 was only lifted over forty years later in 1965 spanning about two generations making it so that new immigrants would have to start to assimilate from square one in fact the pioneer korean americans could barely speak any korean anymore to help the new koreans assimilate anyway residing within the same city they shared ancestry but experienced a time separation meanwhile sessue hayakawa 1886 1973 became the first male sex symbol of hollywood long before and the precursor to rudolph valentino his fame rivaling that of douglas fairbanks and charlie chaplin starting in the silent era with the cheat 1915 directed by cecil b demille hayakawa became a top leading man for romantic dramas in the 1910s and early 1920s he transitioned into sound films talkies including daughter of the dragon 1931 opposite anna may wong he also diversified his body of work with westerns and action films but was mostly typecast as a villain and exotic asian lover that white women could not have in response hayakawa independently produced 23 films and earned 2 million a year most recently he appeared in the bridge on the river kwai 1957 for which he was nominated for both an academy award and golden globe award in acting hayakawa sought to bring muga or the absence of doing to his performances anna may wong 19051961 was an american actress considered to be the first chinese american hollywood movie star and with international recognition while often playing a stereotypical dragon lady role as in the silent douglas fairbanks picture the thief of bagdad 1924 and the josef von sternberg talkie daughter of the dragon 1931 playing opposite hayakawa wong caught the attention of audiences and critics alike american antimiscegenation laws prevented her from sharing an onscreen kiss with any person of another race even if the character was asian but being portrayed by a white actor her roles limited in hollywood wong traveled to europe and became a sensation there starring in films like shanghai express 1932 where many critics say she upstaged her friend marlene dietrich eventually returning to america she became more vocal about chinese roles and acted in some smaller but more lasting films like the daughter of shanghai 1937 later she turned her efforts to the chinese cause against japan including the second sinojapanese war given the incredible success of these asian americans in the early days of hollywood cinema in both silent and sound pictures it gives pause to consider if this contributed to the racebased asian exclusion act of 1924 ii hong kong taiwanese american independents fast forward to when the asians were no longer excluded by law in america 1965 post1965 hong kong and taiwanese americans provided early role models for korean american filmmakers long after the artificial talent drought created by the asian exclusion act of 1924 hong kong american bruce lee would create the basis for a globally popular film genre lees father was a famous cantonese opera actor in hong kong from infancy to age 18 lee had already appeared in twenty films he attended an international school where he would excel at chacha dancing and boxing but as his streetfighting caught up with him lee would make his way across the pacific to seattle where he studied drama and philosophy to san francisco and oakland where opened his first schools then expanding to los angeles teaching to all races chinese martial arts which at the time in america was generally considered inferior to japanese karate teaching nonchinese people was against chinese tradition and lee would have to prevail in a challenge fight for this right he would later devise his own martial art called jeet kun do blending the strengths of all major fighting styles even boxing and fencing doing away with inherent nationalism of styles to promote the individual martial artist above all overwhelmed by the expansion of his schools lee eventually decided that movies would be the best way to broadly spread his martial arts philosophy he would make a name for himself playing kato in the green hornet 1966 which also earned him fame back in hong kong however after warner bros reworked his the warrior concept and renamed it kung fu 1972 casting a different lead lee returned to hong kong where he would go on to to his recordbreaking star debut in the big boss 1971 even more box office success with fist of fury 1972 then full creative control with way of the dragon 1972 hollywood came calling with the coproduction enter the dragon 1973 which would cement his name while the posthumous game of death 1978 containing only scenes shot before his untimely death augmented his legend his fame rising mostly after his passing lee singlehandedly hatched a global phenomenon creating a film industry in hong kong while inspiring countless people across the world to learn chinese martial arts without lee there would be no jackie chan no jet li bruce lee would pass before enjoying his fame but many would exploit his persona to create a new genre of films called bruceploitation productions would give diverse actors spoof bruce lee names with different spellings to promote their own movies in fact the most popular actors included a chinese bruce le a taiwanese going by bruce li and a korean dragon lee lees legend would continue to receive homage treatment in american cult classics like the last dragon 1985 with the african american character bruce leroy however while nonasian and asian leads have proliferated in the martial arts genre another asian american lead has yet to arise since lee hongkong american director wayne wang is a pioneer for having breakout hits in both the independent and mainstream cinemas his gritty blackandwhite 16mm mystery comedy chan is missing 1982 is a simple and ingenious expression of the depth and complexities of the chinese american enclave in san francisco while following protagonists trying to locate a vague character who has disappeared with their money the story of ambiguous identity is smartly employed to relate the experience of being an asian american while keeping the story fresh truly it is a triumph and the cornerstone of modern asian american cinema in his hollywood followup the joy luck club 1989 portraying the struggles and stories of chinese american women in san francisco based on the novel by amy tan wang repeats critical and boxoffice success in the mainstream then an unprecedented achievement for an asian american director however within the community wang received some criticism for the stereotypical depictions of asian american males very differently drawn than in his lowbudget breakout ang lee is a taiwanese filmmaker who studied in the us but with his debut films he demonstrated a passion for exploring the stories of the chinese american experience in pushing hands 1992 a grandfather who teaches tai chi leaves beijing for new york to live with his grandson and white daughterinlaw and must learn to navigate his confucian traditions within american individuality in the wedding banquet 1993 a gay son marries a mainland chinese in ny to placate his parents abroad and cover up his samesex relationship but when they visit him to witness the marriage drama ensues both films deeply mine the intensity of cultural conflicts coming out of the chinese american experience then came justin lin with his sundance breakout and crimedrama better luck tomorrow 2002 aka blt the first film to portray a darker side to asian american characters outside the stereotypical colonial lens loosely based on a true story of korean american students in fullerton ca the film follows a group of overachieving high schoolers who use their good grades as a cover to engage in drugs alcohol sex thievery ultimately they succumb to their envy of an older college guy who has it all too easy and murder him at the movies sundance premiere qa following the screening when a white audience member condemned the negative portrayals of asian americans roger ebert himself defended the film saying what i find very offensive and condescending about your statement is nobody would say to a bunch of white filmmakers how could you do this to your people this film has the right to be about this the main reasons i love this movie are the tone and the main character the tone is one of total futility and unlike other prison films like the shawshank redemption theres a complete absence of hope theres no lesson about enduring and things will turn out okay dont expect any grand justice for the villains or for luke to bring their crimes to light the world of the film almost mirrors our own and in our world prisoners are often the victims of fate and bad luck just like the prisoners here the film reminds us that criminals are still people no matter what they did to end up in jail and attitudes like theyre all scum let them rot in the clink prisoner rights why are we wasting money on this only serve to bolster corrupt prison wardens who love nothing more than to exploit the labor of the incarcerated the tone is gritty futile and real but luke is a character who transcends all this he smiles and chuckles to himself even when under the heel of the warden hes the antithesis to what the warden wants a broken downcast obedient servant he will break rocks and do his part but he does it with the sun captured in his eyes and yes that everlong smirk when luke drops the title you get him completely sometimes nothing can be a real cool hand he had no cards in his situation at all yet still comes out looking smoother and more levelheaded than the warden and no matter what i watched fando y lis 1968 on september 5th and psychomagic a healing art 2019 on december 17th 2021 these four months i have seen 9 jodorowsky pronounced yodorofsky directed projects and viewed them in the order he initially released them the abckoarrow video release was a beautiful box set and got me thinking it was time i took a deeper look into this surrealist giant jodorowsky was someone i made fun of when i was just getting into film i saw holy mountain about 20 years ago and my favorite movie at that time was either shawshank redemption or maybe something like boondock saints not to disparage those im just saying i had not seen a lot of arthouse films at that time holy mountain came off as pretentious disjointed and arrogant just as a quick aside i also hated branded to kill when i saw it around the same time so safe to say my patience for experimental cinema was low so i avoided jodorowsky but in that time i have seen thousands of movies and more importantly come to have a deep love for the films of fellini which he cites as an influence buñuel and gilliam and i decided it was time to give these 9 films a serious look from a collection standpoint tusk has never received a physical release as far as i can tell so i had to use dvdladycom and they found an old french tv release that was very poor quality but was watchable i am happy to forward to anyone if you want to see it fando el topo holy mountain and psychomagic were all released as part of the aforementioned box set severin films put out a beautiful box set of santa sangre and both the dance of reality and endless poetry have individual releases from abcko so that leaves rainbow thief which i had to get from region b thats another one i am happy to send to anyone living in north america just be aware you need a regionfree player i also made the decision to watch his holy trinity plus santa sangre with the directors commentary for me this was absolutely the right decision there is very little dialog in fando el topo and holy mountain so it was easy to follow the story while he was providing context seeing these films through his eyes was a great experience jodorowsky is an open and authentic person he does not shy away from his controversial decisions and very calmly defends his creativity he speaks with such love to these early films that i sincerely believe he was making them with the intent of changing the world the commentary with santa sangre probably would have been better to watch separately as there is a lot more talking and plot but it is definitely worth a listen at several points the moderator is pushing him for why he added visual elements in and he keeps blowing off the questions or changing the topic finally he says that hes a surrealist so he doesnt have to defend these choices it just felt like the right one to make at the time i loved it you can probably read it in my tone if you go through my individual writings on these films but i moved from curious and indifferent to curious and positive to flat out loving jodorowskys films he makes six movies with the central theme of claiming your identity apart from a loved one a parent or a birth family he then makes two films recounting his early years and his ninth film celebrates his own creation of the psychomagic practice i laughed that even at 90 he still felt the need to defend his creation so hard but the film is certainly on brand for jodorowsky and allows us to see a glimpse into don alejandro the prophet not jodorowsky the filmmaker this ranking will most likely change my second time through his catalog but it will be a while before i get to that so i will leave this up here for now and welcome any and all thoughts concerns for my sanity debate here is a link to my scribbles on fando y lis which will directly link to all of the other ones 1 la montaña sagrada the holy mountain 1973 the pursuit of getting in touch with something more powerful than yourself and the importance of finding a family as an adult that will help you get there i love the ambition and the way jodorowsky breaks down every layer of artifice throughout the film 2 santa sangre 1989 letting go of the mother and reclaiming the mother energy the mother represents mother country birth mother as well as jungs anima persona probably jodorowskys most complete film in terms of both story characters visuals and choreography 3 el topo 1970 letting go of the father and reclaiming the father energy the father based on jodorowskys father represents control oppression rigidity and remaining in a childlike state because of suppressed growth 4 poesia sin fin endless poetry 2016 a look back into jodorowskys teen years and young adulthood the dance of reality ends on a boat leaving their hometown as a family and endless poetry ends with jodorowsky by himself on a boat to france to begin his life as a surrealist 5 la danza de la realidad the dance of reality 2013 a look back into his early years this is mostly focused on the hypocrisy and ultimately weakness of his overbearing father 6 fando y lis 1968 loving a partner without becoming attached in an unhealthy way fando drops to for me because it was his toughest film for me to watch and the only one i felt had a bit of a nasty spirit to it i dont like seeing partner abuse even if its representative of something deeper 7 the rainbow thief 1990 if each film has had a theme of letting go of something this film would be shaking the desire for material wealth in exchange for your soul the message comes through but the overall film was a bit inconsistent for me its the only time jodorowsky worked with professional actors in every role and he could not get financing for 23 years so it didnt go well for anyone 8 psychomagic a healing art 2019 a documentary that paints jodorowsky as a saint and a healer it was okay certainly interesting at times to see what each client had to do in order to release the inner pain that had been holding them back but my this is probably staged radar was on high alert throughout 9 tusk 1980 a sweet film but not a finished one i mean its a feature film but jodorowsky has said realized its been a while since ive seen such a thing it feels a bit of a lost art in these days of irony meta selfreference and high concept so two questions for the sub 1 am i correct in thinking this kind of film used to be more common and has become less so at least in the relative mainstream of film is it the rise of genre or just the zeitgeist of everything having to everything having to be done with a wink and a nudge 2 what other movies have a downtoearth character who changes other characters and even the world just by being themselves preferably also with low stakes theyre not saving the world theyre just making it a little better ones that come to mind are amelie 2001 shawshank redemption 1994 and sortof groundhog day 1993 though thats more about him growing to be such a person i believe it was scorsese who said that midbudget films are dying were approaching a time where films either cost 5 million or 150 million to produce the latter being almost exclusively comic book films and rebootsprequelssequels of already successful franchises while this is great news for foreign film makers who could never compete with hollywood blockbusters anyway it does raise an interesting question what does the future of cinema actually look like our viewing habits have changed streaming binge watching youtube social media content etc is stealing precious watch time passive engagement and instant gratification in the form of quick and snappy content is also a part of the new reality this does not mix well with classic cinema which encourages you to sit still for 2 hours broader view i see three key characters that represent broader aspects of prison life the film beautifully showcases how life unfolds in jail let me explain how andy a new fish who has to adapt to the harsh life around him in jail he faces bullying and beatings from the sisters gradually makes friends with his fellow inmates and learns the dynamics of prison life he figures out who his enemies and allies are and understands how to behave in different situations with different people brooks you could call him the elder of the prison he has experienced more than enough of jail lifeso much that he has forgotten what life outside the prison feels like he has lost hope and found his comfort in jail he doesnt want to adapt to life outside and that is his storya story that reflects the reality of inmates who have spent most of their lives in prison red he is neither a newcomer nor a veteran hes somewhere in the middle he knows his way around the prison and represents the life of an inmate who is welladjusted but not institutionalized to the extent of brooks these three characters provide a broader perspective on life in jail each offering a different lens to view it characters andy he doesnt speak much but he knows how to navigate people and situations he is cautious and doesnt speak to red until hes sure its safe andy is resilienthe endures the beatings from the sisters without ever losing his calm he is strategic using his skills to make allies for his own benefit like helping the police save 35000 without paying taxes andy also has a broader vision planning his escape meticulously from the beginning most importantly he never loses hope red a wise and practical man he has established a solid network in prison and knows how to interact with people to his advantage his friendship with andy brings mutual benefits and a better life which is what a wise man doesbuild meaningful connections brooks the scene where brooks struggles to cross the road after being released shows how cruel prison life can be from the outside prison may not seem as harsh but it destroys a person from within that is the tragic reality of rehabilitation for someone like brooks williams tommy he is introduced to accelerate the movies pacing and his death is tragic but not unearned tommy is naïve and doesnt understand peoples motives which makes him vulnerable he pays the price and is killed his death marks a turning point in the movie and while it effectively serves the plot i wonder if it was the best way to reveal the truth about andys innocence nonetheless the plot pays off camera movements and world building the main setting is shawshank prison and the movie explores its key areas through a newcomers perspective from what i observed the film showcases the prison step by step as if through andys eyes when the inmates arrive the camera pans out from above giving viewers a wide shot of the prisons grounds and layout then it shows the cellsthe second thing inmates see after entering shawshank next comes the canteen followed by the work areas by this point the audience feels familiar with the prison later the film introduces the library the wardens cabin and even glimpses of the world outside the camera captures the prison as a whole giving it a distinct identity though the movie focuses primarily on characters and their stories the visual representation of shawshank adds depth and beauty colors the colors in the movie are muted reflecting the bleak and monotonous life inside the prison cast dont get me wrong but most of the actors dont look like celebrities which adds to the realism of the story you wouldnt immediately associate their faces with fame and that makes them more relatable the cast feels like ordinary people which helps build the authentic atmosphere of the prison together their performances create a vivid and believable picture of jail life sounds the sound design is incredibly crisp paying attention to even the smallest details you can hear subtle sounds like the spinning of bullets in a revolver or the flapping of a flag these tiny auditory elements enhance the films atmosphere and show the filmmakers attention to detail i see a lot of people online particularly here on reddit mentioning passion projects films from popular directors and unless theyre a dedicated fan of the film in question will usually discuss it in a degrading or cynical way recent examples like beau is afraid babylon and the new megalopolis are works that can be summed up as the directors behind them enjoying a blank slate to do whatever they want of course results on this may vary for many people these films arent good thats fine but the constant accusing online against directors for having an ego and calling them pretentious because they make a film with no involvement studio execs so they flaunt their stylish muscles feels like weightless criticism because i dont know what they mean most of the time when they use the word ego if if was a real life conversation itd likely be easier to parse out their meaning but because you see it on places like reddit the majority of comments have a snarky ironypoisoned tone that obscures the point theyre making so i just have to assume they use ego like other buzzwords in online film discussions as critiques that have no value if you cant explain why or even how a director is showing off or being egotistical in their movie why should your opinion to be treated seriously if you dont like a choice they made say that but putting a veil of fauxintellectualism over your dislike when you have no substantial point to make is obnoxious this happens a lot within adapted media theres lots of handwaving of any sort of aueterlike behaviour from directors that handle translating original works onto the screen people almost exclusively on social media ive never seen this argument in an academic capacity get really prissy when a deviation from the source material weekorso on reddit has been filled with extensive listmaking like liam neeson i love making lists in my opinion however published or public favourite movie lists have unfortunately devolved into two camps those dedicated to either imdb top 250 picks or sight soundtype films at the expense of sounding derivative the former group seemingly chooses films based purely on entertainmentfactor and audience familiarity and are perceived as lacking in originality the latter camp appears to recognise a film only by its artistic merits almost academically rather than anything else selfimportant and pompous similarly both groups are consensusdriven have you noticed the shawshank redemption has consistently ranked as the best film on imdb whilst citizen kane never seems to leave the top five of sight sounds greatest films lists side note was it just me or did every individual sight sound list look almost identical the issue is essentially reduced to the public nature of these lists and the question of how others perceive us of the sight sound faction critics and film historians appear to be the most guilty is jeanne dielman 23 quai du commerce 1080 bruxelles the best film of all time on the other hand can we really pretend forrest gump ranked eleventh on imdb is a better film than persona not ranked why do you care though its about peoples personal taste right art is subjective yes but part of the cinema experience is discussion consuming and processing a film talking about it or engaging with others opinions of it then seeing if your view of said film changes over time this is unfortunately where consensus forms i admit to having pretended to liking plenty of films because of the many others who enjoyed them on the other hand because of these discussions i found myself digesting cinema outside my comfort zone my education soon expanded beyond filmmaker icons like spielberg towards lesserknown directors like kieślowski i also learned to remove the silver rod and appreciate films that wouldnt appear on any sight sound list i have a soft spot for michael bay whether you only go to a theatre to see the latest marvel movie or you practically live off the criterion collection theres a middle ground between these two supposed worlds in other words the imdbers need to grow a little more and the sightandsounders need to learn how to have some fun maybe its why i find it refreshing to see a filmmakers favourite films include something of an oddball choice for example justin kurzel listed step brothers in his top ten for sight sound i appreciate individuality and consensus is its nemesis many prefer to be right and agreed with rather than acknowledging the art that directly speaks to you the assertion however that art is subjective is difficult to argue identity and experience ultimately inform ones relationship with cinema its partly why film is so important your connection with a film is yours and your favourites tell us something about you as a person perhaps this is my way of saying id rather watch the day after tomorrow over days of heaven by terrence malick whom i adore any day of the week mostly this post is a letter to critics and letterboxd users and rfilm members dont worry about how youre perceived is forrest gump your favourite film is come and see you have both my blessing and my love with top tens i dont want to be agreed with i want to learn more and i want to see you ps i want to note that due to the nature of professional film criticism its natural to see why consensus forms amongst critics when you sometimes see hundreds of films a year you can make the argument critics know more than the regular filmgoer that being said the time and place of reviewing a film whats happening in the historical moment can impact said consensus last time i checked american hustle had a 90 on metacritic i cant think of a worse film to be rated so highly apologies to american hustle enthusiasts im hollywood december 11th 2024 the pianist december 13th 2024 personally i would pick 12 angry men and the shawshank redemption broader view i see three key characters that represent broader aspects of prison life the film beautifully showcases how life unfolds in jail let me explain how andy a new fish who has to adapt to the harsh life around him in jail he faces bullying and beatings from the sisters gradually makes friends with his fellow inmates and learns the dynamics of prison life he figures out who his enemies and allies are and understands how to behave in different situations with different people brooks you could call him the elder of the prison he has experienced more than enough of jail lifeso much that he has forgotten what life outside the prison feels like he has lost hope and found his comfort in jail he doesnt want to adapt to life outside and that is his storya story that reflects the reality of inmates who have spent most of their lives in prison red he is neither a newcomer nor a veteran hes somewhere in the middle he knows his way around the prison and represents the life of an inmate who is welladjusted but not institutionalized to the extent of brooks these three characters provide a broader perspective on life in jail each offering a different lens to view it characters andy he doesnt speak much but he knows how to navigate people and situations he is cautious and doesnt speak to red until hes sure its safe andy is resilienthe endures the beatings from the sisters without ever losing his calm he is strategic using his skills to make allies for his own benefit like helping the police save 35000 without paying taxes andy also has a broader vision planning his escape meticulously from the beginning most importantly he never loses hope red a wise and practical man he has established a solid network in prison and knows how to interact with people to his advantage his friendship with andy brings mutual benefits and a better life which is what a wise man doesbuild meaningful connections brooks the scene where brooks struggles to cross the road after being released shows how cruel prison life can be from the outside prison may not seem as harsh but it destroys a person from within that is the tragic reality of rehabilitation for someone like brooks williams tommy he is introduced to accelerate the movies pacing and his death is tragic but not unearned tommy is naïve and doesnt understand peoples motives which makes him vulnerable he pays the price and is killed his death marks a turning point in the movie and while it effectively serves the plot i wonder if it was the best way to reveal the truth about andys innocence nonetheless the plot pays off camera movements and world building the main setting is shawshank prison and the movie explores its key areas through a newcomers perspective from what i observed the film showcases the prison step by step as if through andys eyes when the inmates arrive the camera pans out from above giving viewers a wide shot of the prisons grounds and layout then it shows the cellsthe second thing inmates see after entering shawshank next comes the canteen followed by the work areas by this point the audience feels familiar with the prison later the film introduces the library the wardens cabin and even glimpses of the world outside the camera captures the prison as a whole giving it a distinct identity though the movie focuses primarily on characters and their stories the visual representation of shawshank adds depth and beauty colors the colors in the movie are muted reflecting the bleak and monotonous life inside the prison cast dont get me wrong but most of the actors dont look like celebrities which adds to the realism of the story you wouldnt immediately associate their faces with fame and that makes them more relatable the cast feels like ordinary people which helps build the authentic atmosphere of the prison together their performances create a vivid and believable picture of jail life sounds the sound design is incredibly crisp paying attention to even the smallest details you can hear subtle sounds like the spinning of bullets in a revolver or the flapping of a flag these tiny auditory elements enhance the films atmosphere and show the filmmakers attention to detail movies avengers endgame interstellar whiplash rocky lll trey parker now that the movie is 20 years old i wanted to go over my history with the film the plot and why i consider it one of my favorite movies my first exposure to this movie was completely by accident i was in middle school at the time my class i was in was in the computer lab and one of the kids was on the apple movie trailers website one of the trailers he was watching was team america he got in trouble for watching a trailer for rrated movie but i looked over to see what this was about when i saw the puppets and model sets in the trailer my reaction to this was like wait a minute this is just like thunderbirds it was not what i expected not that many people in my class knew what this style of puppetry was i did because i grew up on thunderbirds it was the first show that i saw you know what i was doing when team america came out i was trying to find the entire show on dvd along with other puppet shows supermarionation like captain scarlet and joe 90 which were all created by the same person gerry anderson but they were hard to find at least where i lived but youre telling me all i had to do was go to the cinema and see a new supermarionationstyle film and this wasnt a coincidence trey and matt were inspired by thunderbirds for team america but werent fans of the show however they stated that they liked the puppet and model work of the show i wouldnt see the movie until it was released on dvd and immediately i was hooked for those who havent seen it the plot involves a police force called team america whose mission is to stop terrorists unfortunately this police force does more harm than good destroying everything insight to stop the terrorists which causes the public to disown them for their reckless ways especially the film actors guild one are the members was killed on a mission and they recruited an actor named gary johnson to take over using his acting skills to infiltrate the terrorist group but dictator kim jong il is planning an even deadlier attack on the world using weapons of mass destruction the puppet and model sets are definitely on par with the works of gerry anderson and as a thunderbirds fan i got a lot of the nods and references to the series but while being a thunderbirds fan helps to appreciate the film its not a requirement to understand the movie just like you dont have to be a fan of star trek like me to enjoy galaxy quest now lets get to the stuff i like about the film the attention to detail its characters its music its jokes to me this is a comedic gem this movie just throws jokes at you and 99 of them hit bullseye the main humor of the film is that its a parody of bigbudget summer blockbusters with jokes poking fun at overthetop explosions cliché speeches tragic backstories all things you would see in a michael bay and roland emmerich movie now in the wrong hands this movie could have been a complete disaster it could have just been some generic raunchy rrated comedy that would try to be offensive and gross for no reason other than just a cheap laugh however while trey and matts style of humor can be vulgar they are not stupid people both guys know how to be raunchy and make it work and these two guys understand how jokes and punchlines work south park is an obvious example of that team america is basically south park as if it was independence day or armageddon of the characters in this movie spottswoode is my favorite he has the best dialogue and quotable lines and he pretty much steals every scene hes in the runnerup is kim jong il for the same reasons as spottswoode as for the members of team america my favorite is chris hes the character that goes through an arc he despises gary and all the actors for what happened to him when he went to see cats on broadway yeah insert cats movie joke to accepting gary after he saves his life from susan sarandon in my opinion the best scene in the movie is garys speech toward the end the dicks pussies and assholes speech im not gonna get political but i think this speech is more relevant now than it has ever been and of course i cant talk about the movie without talking about the soundtrack even if you havent seen the movie you know the team america theme song america fuck yeah its definitely catchy you and a memorable song in the movie and the rest of the songs are just as catchy and memorable i cant even do them justice youll have to listen to them for yourself if i do have one minor issue with the movie and its something a lot of people bring up its the puppet sex scene as much as i like this film i will agree that i could have done without that and its usually the part i skip over when watching the film although i did like the song that played overall south park and thunderbirds are two of my useful is it recency bias comes up as a flaw almost without fail and thats understandable it can be very frustrating to see older classics that have stood the test of time get overlooked in favor of and surpassed in rating by newer films that you perhaps rightfully so feel arent as good now i think its prevalence has been at times considerably overstated but even beyond that id like to argue that if not necessarily an entirely good thing recency bias has its upsides mainly i question what the real use of the list is if you say its to catalogue what the best 250 movies ever made are that may be the platonic ideal but it obviously cant achieve its goal because there is no correct answer to this we can compare and argue about acting editing screenwriting directing etc until the cows come home but at the end of the day you cant empirically prove to me that the shawshank redemption is better than pirates of the caribbean everyone will have their own list and all we can do is contrast and compare and ideally come out the other end with more understanding of and appreciation for the medium and our fellow enthusiasts so what does it actually do it represents an aggregate of the communitys taste thats the thing barring any adjustment by the weighting process the list truly dare i say objectively accomplishes and knowing the communitys taste has a couple of benefits if youre just starting out getting interested in film its an excellent jumpingoff point to find movies that are worth watching for one reason or another it includes a large collection of films throughout history that for a variety of reasons are deemed culturally significant by a lot of people as well as good you get pointed in the direction of films you might otherwise have never even heard of that get revitalized in popularity by their inclusion it also fosters a sense of community these are the movies popular on this specific site a lot of them are obviously considered classics by the general public but there are more obscure and unique picks as well as a result of this sense of community it offers a common framework for conversation experiencing at least a couple of entries in the top 250 can be a great primer to get you engaged with other letterboxd users as well as film discourse at large and i think the list has a recency bias at least in part because these discussions have a recency bias of course its skewed because a lot of it is in the context of this very bias but i feel like i see more discussion of films that came out within the last 10 years than a lot of classics you might object that this is what the popularity metric should be for but this way you get a nice mix of quality as well as recency now you may say that the top 250 list should be purely for percieved quality and i dont think recency bias is an entirely good thing but consider this and i just want to say this is a hot take in the original sense i didnt research this or anything its just a feeling i think there may be some sort of classicbias going on as well what i mean is that this status as an alltime great film can be selfperpetuating its on the list as well as many others because its great and its great because its on all these lists and thats not to say that any of these movies are necessarily undeserving but i worry that this seeming aversion to new movies entering the list could lead to a very stale canon i sometimes think about how weird it is that the music we think of as a canon or great works of art are almost all hundres of years old even though never has more new music and more diversity and genres been released every year than now i really doubt that music just across the board hasnt been as good since the 19th century nobodys taking old greats away from you except for streaming services that would rather delete their catalogue than pay taxes maybe and even if some of the new entries might not be as deserving in many peoples eyes i feel like some freshness and evolution is at least not all bad i will concede however that this also comes at the expense of more obscure movies that deserve more recognition which is a shame one last note i see people here trying to calculate recency bias by stacking up top entries per decase or something like that and i understand the approach but i think it may be a little misguided theres so much you cant easily quantify in there from the fact that so many more movies are released today than decades ago to changes in accessibility both good like how equipment and knowhow is perhaps more accessible to laypeople than ever before and bad like how new business models and changes in revenue streams heavily impacted who gets funded and largely killed midbudget movies and then theres the fact that not only is knowledge more accessible new filmmakers can learn from the failures and successes of their predecessors or how taste and whats considered good isnt and shouldnt be innate or immutable and can change over time im excited by what new movies can do and are doing and if eyeballs on them come at some expense to old classics who have had their spot in the limelight and discussions for decades thats at least not a wholly bad tradeoff and please stop bringing up into the spiderverse when the topic comes up its over 5 years old at this point it deserves its spot as much as the other entries i finally watched the shawshank redemption after someone recommended it to me as a movie about hope so i went in expecting something gentle and uplifting i only knew it was number one on imdb and had something to do with a prison but that whole gentle movie idea went straight out the window basically right after the court scene there will be spoilers from here on out spoilers alert so the first prison sequence really threw me a guy asks when do we eat and the guard says you eat when we say you eat you piss when we say you piss and you shit when we say you shit and then hits him hard and thats just the beginning later that night another prisoner gets beaten to death just for panicking and yelling for help movie doens get easier more violence and repeated rapes follow soon after when i saw level of sadism from the guards and the warden had me thinking the whole hope theme was going to be tiny little moments of andy learning to survive by allying with these people the rooftop scene was the first hint i figured that was the message then stuff started happening that didnt quite line up with that idea like how this super tightly controlled prison somehow allowed a full library music etc and these moments of strange freedom still i was like okay this could makes sense but then the ending came nd the whole miraculous escape completely floored me it almost felt like a dream sequence almost like a fairy tale the coincidences stacking up so neatly and dramaticaly that a part of me was like come on i mean im not someone who nitpicks plot holes but i couldnt stop thinking about how a prison that strict supposedly never checked behind a poster or how a guy could dig a tunnel for almost 20 years without being caught or how easily he moves around afterward when he is out but i guess thats exactly why the movie resonates so much because thats what hope is at the heart of it its hope against all odds hope is basically useless when things make sense and you can logically expect things to go your way it matters when everything doesnt sometimes its the only thing left like when someone has a terminal illness with a 1 survival rate and ends up living decades or when someone in poverty suddenly got a a brilliant business idea and actually becomes that oneinamillion success story its irrational impossible until it isnt looking at the movie from that angle the fairytale aspects suddenly felt intentional and kind of essential they give the movie that mythical biggerthanlife feel that clearly so many people connect with curious to hear what others thought of the movie i finally sat down to rewatch the shawshank redemption 1994 dir frank darabont after skipping it for over a decade with all the remakes and reboots flooding theaters these days i wondered if this 90s classic would hold up spoiler it doesnt just hold upit floors you what struck me hardest this time was how the films structure mirrors real resilience andy dufresne tim robbins doesnt escape shawshank through some flashy plot twist its years of quiet deliberate acts chipping away at that wall with a rock hammer playing mozart over the pa system forging bonds in the laundry room that rooftop rain scene its not just cathartic its a masterclass in visual symbolism the water washing away the grime isnt subtleits a baptism a rejection of the institutionalization red morgan freeman warns about in a world of quickfix superhero arcs shawshank reminds us hope isnt a superpower its erosion slow painful but inevitable if you keep showing up at 34 crawling through my own five hundred yards of shit lost my dad career pivots the usual adulting hell that line from red get busy living or get busy dying landed like a gut punch but beyond the feels its fascinating how darabont adapts stephen kings novella he amps up the friendship between andy and red turning a prison tale into a profound meditation on male vulnerability no wonder its still imdbs toprated its universal without a girl when he was getting arrested as he understands very well what the prisoners are going to do to him afterward i think now there are laws to prevent prison guards from being imprisoned in the prison they once guarded after committing crimes but maybe it was really possible in those days or its just my hope first i just have to say shawshank redemption is my favorite movie of all time it is a true masterpiece i can never get tired of rewatching it if its on no matter at what point i will watch it to the end with that out of the way im surprised it took me so long to read the actual source material by stephen king king is obviously a master author and while hes most well known for his horror novels some of his finest work i would argue are outside that genre the green mile and shawshank sticking out specifically the book is quite easy to read coming in at under 130 pages but the story as told by red flows really well there are no wasted words and stephen is masterful in his craft i was somewhat surprised that so much of the dialog in the movie specifically between red and andy is pretty much exactly as written in the book i mean its hard to beat perfection so its understandable frank darabont chose not to change a lot of the dialog with that said there are numerous differences the big ones being red was actually irish andy was supposed to be short they went through i think 3 or 4 wardens a lot of things with timeline either as far as dialog or events sort of get flipped around but a lot of this i can see why frank did it the source material is amazing by itself but if anything i dont see where someone could complain with franks choices the book did go into a bit more detail about when andy was being victimized by the sisters including the sodomy and bleeding which i think was fair to leave out of the movie also king talked about some child rapist making beautiful figurines through red which darabont also left out one of the biggest set of changes was just having the inmate who would have gotten andy a new trial just moved to a different prison instead of executed this was a fine change by darabont i think as it made it more brutal for andy also in the book the final warden ends up getting off pretty light only stepping down after 3 years with no seeming consequences for all the illegal things he had been doing i think darabont having him kill himself and also having andy mail the incriminating evidence was a very good change finally perhaps one thing that i believe was an improvement by darabont was making it so that andy ended up creating his fake persona by himself instead of needing outside help this adds a bit to andys mystique but man if you like the movie i highly recommend reading the book kings writing of red is so well done his writing on the exploits of andy just makes him so special i have so much respect for both king and darabont usually its pretty clear if the book or movie is better almost always the book wins but i think in this case they are both masterpieces i love movies with ambiguous endings roger dodger and 25th hour spring to mind if you like you can include thelma and louise or butch cassidy and the sundance kid although i doubt thres much debate about the fate of those characters what movies do you think should have ended just before they did making for an improved and ambiguous ending ill listening to old cassettes taking pictures of trees but the more you watch the more you realize how much peace and depth there is behind his routine one thing that stood out to me was how he smiles at the sky every morning like hes genuinely grateful for another day no rush no complaints justexisting with full heart he doesnt talk much unless necessary but when he does interact like with his niece or that random guy he plays shadow games with you can feel how much he enjoys human connection too hes not avoiding peoplehe just doesnt need noise to be happy theres this beautiful bittersweetness hes not choosing loneliness in a sad wayits more like life took him down this path and instead of fighting it he embraced it with full acceptance thats powerful in todays world where everyone feels pressure to chase something constantly the scene where his niece leaves and he quietly criesman that hit different not because hes weak or regretting but because even the strongest hearts carry a godfather shawshank redemption with all the problems of the academy the nominees each year provide an opportunity to see films that would otherwise go unnoticed the 609 nominees that are available to watch from renting buying or other means dont provide the most exciting journey through film history but it does show clear lines of changing norms evolving attitudes and the innovations in tech the patriot is a lost film and the white parade can only be viewed at the ucla film archive which is unfortunate because i suspect the patriot would be the majority favorite of its year nowadays at least half of the films are great and the half that arent have many good films among them the nominees include many firsts for film history usually the films were good to support that recognition big surprises include a thousand clowns dead end stage door the heiress the snake pit sounder a soldiers story the emigrants breaking away ship of fools and an unmarried woman you can come across these films by following an actor or directors filmography but these are films that get left out of relevant conversations in social commentary feminist films black representation etc while the academy is rightfully criticized for their conflicts with guilds and unions their out of touch mentalities and their snobbery toward genres like horror they still picked films that shine a light on the hypocrisy of americans and the complicated life during romantic times i wouldnt have ever thought about the fight in removing the illegitimate classification on birth certificates but blossoms in the dust tells that story you get your heavy dose of war films during and after world war ii but they arent all simple america is great kill the nazis narratives the darkness within small towns and suburbia is explored relatively early in films like kings row and peyton place the films that have outlived the nominees in popularity have done so for a good reason but the bad parts of the oscar nominees eg yellowface blackface questionable morals are worth some viewings for that uncomfortable history and to know firsthand how they operated ive seen most of these once and many of them from at least 10
